frames.caption: Invert the tree: at every node, swap its left and right children, recursively. The result is the mirror image.
frames.note: Inverting a tree means reflecting it left-to-right. The whole job reduces to one rule applied at every node — swap its two children — so a single recursive function over the tree structure does it. We will walk the example tree top-down, swapping as we go.

frames.caption: Visit node 5. It is not null, so swap its two children and recurse into each.
frames.note: Structural recursion: the work at THIS node is just one swap, and the same function handles every node below it. We swap the two child pointers, then trust the recursive calls to invert each (now-swapped) subtree. There is no clever bookkeeping — the shape of the recursion mirrors the shape of the tree, which is exactly why it is "the simplest structural recursion".

frames.caption: Swap node 5's children: left ↔ right. The subtree under 5 is now mirrored; recurse to invert each side.
frames.note: Structural recursion: the work at THIS node is just one swap, and the same function handles every node below it. We swap the two child pointers, then trust the recursive calls to invert each (now-swapped) subtree. There is no clever bookkeeping — the shape of the recursion mirrors the shape of the tree, which is exactly why it is "the simplest structural recursion".

frames.caption: Visit node 3. It is not null, so swap its two children and recurse into each.
frames.note: Structural recursion: the work at THIS node is just one swap, and the same function handles every node below it. We swap the two child pointers, then trust the recursive calls to invert each (now-swapped) subtree. There is no clever bookkeeping — the shape of the recursion mirrors the shape of the tree, which is exactly why it is "the simplest structural recursion".

frames.caption: Swap node 3's children: left ↔ right. The subtree under 3 is now mirrored; recurse to invert each side.
frames.note: Structural recursion: the work at THIS node is just one swap, and the same function handles every node below it. We swap the two child pointers, then trust the recursive calls to invert each (now-swapped) subtree. There is no clever bookkeeping — the shape of the recursion mirrors the shape of the tree, which is exactly why it is "the simplest structural recursion".

frames.caption: Visit node 1. It is not null, so swap its two children and recurse into each.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so swapping them is a no-op — the node is already its own mirror. We still "visit" it to confirm the recursion reached the bottom of this branch before backtracking.

frames.caption: Node 1 is a leaf (both children null) — swapping is a no-op. Mark it visited and unwind.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so swapping them is a no-op — the node is already its own mirror. We still "visit" it to confirm the recursion reached the bottom of this branch before backtracking.

frames.caption: Subtree rooted at 1 is fully inverted. Return 1 up to its parent.
frames.note: The base case: an empty subtree (a null child) has nothing to mirror, so we return immediately. Every leaf bottoms out here twice (its two null children), and these returns are what let the recursion unwind back up the tree.

frames.caption: Visit node 4. It is not null, so swap its two children and recurse into each.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so swapping them is a no-op — the node is already its own mirror. We still "visit" it to confirm the recursion reached the bottom of this branch before backtracking.

frames.caption: Node 4 is a leaf (both children null) — swapping is a no-op. Mark it visited and unwind.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so swapping them is a no-op — the node is already its own mirror. We still "visit" it to confirm the recursion reached the bottom of this branch before backtracking.

frames.caption: Subtree rooted at 4 is fully inverted. Return 4 up to its parent.
frames.note: The base case: an empty subtree (a null child) has nothing to mirror, so we return immediately. Every leaf bottoms out here twice (its two null children), and these returns are what let the recursion unwind back up the tree.

frames.caption: Subtree rooted at 3 is fully inverted. Return 3 up to its parent.
frames.note: The base case: an empty subtree (a null child) has nothing to mirror, so we return immediately. Every leaf bottoms out here twice (its two null children), and these returns are what let the recursion unwind back up the tree.

frames.caption: Visit node 8. It is not null, so swap its two children and recurse into each.
frames.note: Structural recursion: the work at THIS node is just one swap, and the same function handles every node below it. We swap the two child pointers, then trust the recursive calls to invert each (now-swapped) subtree. There is no clever bookkeeping — the shape of the recursion mirrors the shape of the tree, which is exactly why it is "the simplest structural recursion".

frames.caption: Swap node 8's children: left ↔ right. The subtree under 8 is now mirrored; recurse to invert each side.
frames.note: Structural recursion: the work at THIS node is just one swap, and the same function handles every node below it. We swap the two child pointers, then trust the recursive calls to invert each (now-swapped) subtree. There is no clever bookkeeping — the shape of the recursion mirrors the shape of the tree, which is exactly why it is "the simplest structural recursion".

frames.caption: Visit node 9. It is not null, so swap its two children and recurse into each.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so swapping them is a no-op — the node is already its own mirror. We still "visit" it to confirm the recursion reached the bottom of this branch before backtracking.

frames.caption: Node 9 is a leaf (both children null) — swapping is a no-op. Mark it visited and unwind.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so swapping them is a no-op — the node is already its own mirror. We still "visit" it to confirm the recursion reached the bottom of this branch before backtracking.

frames.caption: Subtree rooted at 9 is fully inverted. Return 9 up to its parent.
frames.note: The base case: an empty subtree (a null child) has nothing to mirror, so we return immediately. Every leaf bottoms out here twice (its two null children), and these returns are what let the recursion unwind back up the tree.

frames.caption: Subtree rooted at 8 is fully inverted. Return 8 up to its parent.
frames.note: The base case: an empty subtree (a null child) has nothing to mirror, so we return immediately. Every leaf bottoms out here twice (its two null children), and these returns are what let the recursion unwind back up the tree.

frames.caption: Subtree rooted at 5 is fully inverted. Return 5 up to its parent.
frames.note: The base case: an empty subtree (a null child) has nothing to mirror, so we return immediately. Every leaf bottoms out here twice (its two null children), and these returns are what let the recursion unwind back up the tree.

frames.caption: Every node has been swapped — the tree is fully inverted (the mirror image of the original).
frames.note: Every node has been visited and its children swapped, so the whole tree is now the mirror image of the original: at each level, left and right are flipped. Each node is touched exactly once, giving O(n) time, and the recursion stack costs O(h) for the tree height.
